Making out and eating pizza with J.Crew

Making out and eating pizza with J.Crew

readfeedme.com · by Emily SundbergSep 04, 2024

Today\xe2\x80\x99s letter is free, and brought to you by J.Crew.

Good morning everyone. I am slightly hungover from an unnecessary (but fun!) extra glass of wine last night at Gage & Tollner. Last night, we had a Feed Me book club for paid readers in Brooklyn \xe2\x80\x94 thank you everyone who came, and talked about sex, divorce, and dating in New York. I\xe2\x80\x99ve been drinking Magna every time I drink but I ran out\xe2\x80\xa6

The best September issue cover belongs to..J.Crew.

J.Crew\xe2\x80\x99s iconic catalog ceased production in 2017, but in a genius marketing move they\xe2\x80\x99ve brought it back. You might be wondering why the brand would spend so much time, money, and production on an analog advertising strategy, but I need to point out a few things. Erika wrote an ode to the characters in the catalog back in May \xe2\x80\x94 it blew up on Substack. The @lostjcrew account on Instagram has over 80k followers. The comment section on a recent Sprezza post about J.Crew catalogs is a study in the brand itself \xe2\x80\x94 one of them reads:

\xe2\x80\x9cLove how almost all of these are outdoors. In the city, at the beach, on a boat; it\xe2\x80\x99s a stark contrast to today. How many brands today are doing their look books / ads against a colored background or a wall with a chair and side table against it? Most of them? I understand that there\xe2\x80\x99s pros to doing it that way but a lot of these older photo books showed you their clothing in action, worn out and about while doing things that pertained to the brands legacy. J Crew was a lifestyle brand and as such the ads reflected that at a time where people weren\xe2\x80\x99t glued to their phones. Look books were reflecting what people did: living life doing things. Today it\xe2\x80\x99s all about a fit pic in a mirror so it\xe2\x80\x99s no surprise we get look books with guys just standing there in a pose; it\xe2\x80\x99s reflective of what people are doing today.\xe2\x80\x9d

Brand loyalists in the J.Crew Passport Program will get an issue of the catalog straight to their mailbox, but you can also grab a copy in-store, or request one online. I guess you could also steal your neighbors copy if you see it in your vestibule, but I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t recommend that on the record (although it\xe2\x80\x99s tempting).

So now you understand why the medium is important. But let\xe2\x80\x99s get to the message.

The first thing you\xe2\x80\x99ll notice about the J.Crew Fall 2024 cover (the one I received \xe2\x80\x93 there are four collectible versions in circulation) is Demi Moore\xe2\x80\x99s long dark hair, surrounding one of the coolest faces in Hollywood. Inside, an interview with the actor reveals that she has been collecting J.Crew pieces for decades, and her collection includes everything from work boots to oversized sweaters. She even discusses the big secret of her industry: they all live in two worlds. The one that plays out on the red carpet, and the one that happens on the grass.

The issue also contains photos that are purely for the campaign \xe2\x80\x93 it\xe2\x80\x99s up to the reader to make up their stories. The group of strangers (or have they known each other forever?) who ended up in a beach house together. The couple who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep their hands off each other in Central Park. And one of my favorites, a two-page spread of varying J.Crew tags from 1983 to today.

The issue also features:

  • 5 dogs

  • 1 slice of New York pizza

  • 2 makeouts

  • 10 barn jackets (I got this one)

  • 1 boat, 1 truck, and 1 taxi \xe2\x80\x94 the proper ratio of transportation in one\xe2\x80\x99s life

This sweater hasn\xe2\x80\x99t come off my shoulders this week, I wore these heels to my meeting with my money guy on Park Avenue last week and walked out of the office learning that subscription businesses file taxes differently, and this is the shirt I\xe2\x80\x99m wearing at the top of the letter.

Miranda July answered your questions!

Last night, a group of paid Feed Me readers joined me and my friend Krithika in the (beautiful\xe2\x80\xa6) garden behind Warby Parker in Boerum Hill to discuss Miranda July\xe2\x80\x99s All Fours. I was going to try to record our conversation, but decided to keep our conversations off-the-record.

Earlier this summer, some of you submitted questions for Miranda that she kindly took the time to answer, so I have those for you today. Cool for those of you who read the book, perhaps interesting for those of you who want to learn more about writing a \xe2\x80\x9cbook of the summer.\xe2\x80\x9d

How has she approached the question of \xe2\x80\x9cso\xe2\x80\xa6how much of this book is your real experience?\xe2\x80\x9d - does it bug her? Does she understand the inclination or resent people for asking?

A. I knew there would be this question (I would have the same question as a reader) and I thought, Well rather than resent the conflation and work overtime to make an overt distinction between me and the narrator, perhaps I could share a few details of my position with her. Maybe that\xe2\x80\x99s a more fun way to address this problem, to use it as a tool to help tell the fictional story, to add a little energy. I thought of it like I was generously loaning some parts of myself to my narrator and then doing whatever the hell I wanted with her.

Whenever I\xe2\x80\x99m reading \xe2\x80\x9cauto fiction\xe2\x80\x9d (currently If You\xe2\x80\x99re A Girl by Ann Rower) I love thinking This isn\xe2\x80\x99t just some made-up writerly bullshit, this person lived to tell the tale! I wanted that sense of truth and though I knew it had to be fiction, that\xe2\x80\x99s my bag, I also wanted to communicate to the reader that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t just safely dreaming up tall tales from my cozy armchair \xe2\x80\x94 in my own (more complex, more real-life) way I, too, was in the depths.

Does the personal nature of people\xe2\x80\x99s responses to the book change her own perception of the work?

A. I do see the book a little differently now but maybe this always happens when the readers finish the book. I guess because it\xe2\x80\x99s only the readers who can place it in now, a shared now \xe2\x80\x94 try as you might, you can\xe2\x80\x99t do that as just one person. These responses, these stories of how the book has intersected with the reader\xe2\x80\x99s specific lives, is what I hoped for every day of those four years of writing and living and feeling so alone, but placing all my bets on the hunch that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t alone.

The motel room setting was SOOO well written and juicy and fun. I loved the details of the tile, the scent, etc etc. what was the inspiration behind having a setting play such a big part in the book? it's almost like another character. and i would genuinely love to know what the most fun part of writing this book was...torment obviously plays a big role in the plot but it seems like a fun book to write (hope this isn't too earnest and dumb, haha)

A. It was fun to decorate the hotel room, to write those scenes. I had never done anything like that in real life and it seemed indulgent, like a pleasure I had given myself \xe2\x80\x94 that was the initial impetus, nothing deeper. It was only much later (yesterday?) that I thought Ohhh\xe2\x80\xa6.she\xe2\x80\x99s making a home. And maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s a book about making a home, a life for yourself that is so specific that it actually makes no sense to anyone else and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have to. If I had known I was doing that at the start I probably wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have done it, it would have seemed like hard, painful work that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a clue about. Sometimes maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s best, in art and writing, to be as dumb as possible, follow the joy, because at the very least that's real. And there, you\xe2\x80\x99ve taken the first step towards the truth.

Remember the scene when they have a brunch for Caro? That\xe2\x80\x99s the only scene where that small character talks and I remember being so surprised by her voice and the things she said, such that it was very easy to write the narrator being taken by surprise. That was fun to write. The tampon scene was too. I wrote that scene bit by bit over about four days, like building a tower that you hope won\xe2\x80\x99t fall before you\xe2\x80\x99re done.

I listened to the entire All Fours audiobook in 2 days, was hooked. What was the approach to recording it? On the one hand it\xe2\x80\x99s just a straight reading of the book, but the performance of certain scenes felt so considered, and the vocal changes for the different characters was so fun.

A. A thing I did with All Fours that I had never done before was that for the final three drafts -- the stage where you are just making tiny (but I would say important) changes \xe2\x80\x94 I read the whole book aloud. To no one; just alone in my studio, about 50 pages a day. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t give great performances or anything, I just read like this so that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t miss anything. Like if it didn\xe2\x80\x99t roll off the tongue, I paused to study why that was, if it was worth it.

I remember telling the producer beforehand that \xe2\x80\x9cI always read very deadpan, I don\xe2\x80\x99t do voices.\xe2\x80\x9d But then about thirty minutes in, I stopped mid-sentence and said from the booth \xe2\x80\x9cHold on \xe2\x80\x94 have I been doing voices?? This is a big problem because I can\xe2\x80\x99t keep this up, and how will I come up with new voices for each new character?!\xe2\x80\x9d But it was too late now, I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to have to go back to the start, and also\xe2\x80\xa6the voices were just coming. Not like big actor-y characters (I mean, you tell me actually; I'm just going off memory here) but each character\xe2\x80\x99s voice seemed fairly obvious. Maybe from those three times reading it aloud.

What was pinned to the \xe2\x80\x9cgarage wall\xe2\x80\x9d when you worked on this project? Other than the wedding portrait (I read the New Yorker piece).

A. I don\xe2\x80\x99t actually have a lot of visual stuff around when I\xe2\x80\x99m writing fiction. The wedding portrait\xe2\x80\xa6that\xe2\x80\x99s the cover of an iBook that the journalist spied, but that shelf is a bit of a time capsule. Actually the whole studio is in a way, a place I\xe2\x80\x99ve worked for 20 years but don\xe2\x80\x99t spend a lot of time decorating. But during those four years of writing I had a rolling rack of clothes/costumes that I would sometimes change into when I wanted a writing break. I\xe2\x80\x99d put on an outfit that felt inspirational and then I'd move my body. Sometimes I\xe2\x80\x99d have to watch a dance video first, something on IG \xe2\x80\x94 Sharon Eyal, Storyboard P \xe2\x80\x94 to remember what dance was (Oh right, you can move any way, you can find a new way to move) and then I\xe2\x80\x99d put on some music and give it a whirl. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t think that much about the relationship between these breaks and dance in the book, but I guess that\xe2\x80\x99s the closest thing I have to the narrator\xe2\x80\x99s clues pinned to the wall.

Why include Kris?

A. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want the change in the marriage to solve all her problems. At best I think a change in structure like that allows you to see yourself more clearly and what you see is kind of sobering. Not wrong, still interesting and often exciting, but suddenly the scope of the issue changes and the clarity can be a little hellish at first, before the new perspective has been integrated. That\xe2\x80\x99s real life to me, and I don\xe2\x80\x99t think it ever ends and...it\xe2\x80\x99s a human right! Taking away someone\xe2\x80\x99s right to transform is such a crime.

Also the strangeness of the first new relationship after a very long marriage ends or changes \xe2\x80\x94 that\xe2\x80\x99s the sort of thing I would have wanted to read about in my early forties; it was something I couldn\xe2\x80\x99t really conceive of.

If we had a water cooler, I\xe2\x80\x99d talk to you about:

  • Back in the spring, I got breakfast with

    Melanie Masarin
    and she told me she was developing a new Ghia product. Le Fizz, a bubble orange blossom-meets strawberry bottle, launched today. I texted Melanie about how she imagines people drinking the bottles (which I imagine will end up on many a chic Thanksgiving bar this fall) and she said, \xe2\x80\x9cBringing a bottle of Ghia and including non-alcoholic options is \xe2\x80\x9cvery mindful\xe2\x80\x9d and we\xe2\x80\x99re excited to have a bottle that is ready-to-share, with only good things in it, for the price of bottle of wine. It\xe2\x80\x99s also tannic, tangy, but not bitter, while still having no added sugar - something very important to me - so I think a lot of people who didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the taste of our initial flavor will really enjoy this one! It hits the spot when you\xe2\x80\x99d be craving wine but it\xe2\x80\x99s absolutely not trying to be wine or non-alcoholic wine.\xe2\x80\x9d I also heard a rumor that the Ghia team hand-labeled 12,000 bottles to bring this launch to life \xe2\x80\x94 not easy launching a business!

  • Chlo\xc3\xab Sevigny is on the cover of The Cut. Emily Gould interviewed her (\xe2\x80\x9cI try to fill my life with other things I can do that help keep those juices flowing and engaged \xe2\x80\x94 to satisfy my urges and also just to be busy.\xe2\x80\x9d), and Nadia Lee Cohen shot the photos.

  • Miami\xe2\x80\x99s swanky restaurant and members\xe2\x80\x99 club ($4,300/year), Casa Tua, opened in New York at The Surrey uptown. artnet\xe2\x80\x99s Annie Armstrong interviewed Maya Fuchs-Bortolami who will be helping to curate the art there.

  • Haliey Welch, AKA Hawk Tuah Girl, launched a podcast. It\xe2\x80\x99s called Talk Tuah.

  • Puck\xe2\x80\x99s Lauren Sherman spoke to Emily Oberg about the Sporty and Rich newsletter I wrote last week and almost gave me a heart attack.

  • This album fucks.

  • The business of mail-order birth control is booming as abortion restrictions become tighter in the US. \xe2\x80\x9cTelehealth service Nurx saw a 1,700% increase in the number of emergency contraception requests, over half of which came from states in the South like Texas, Florida and Tennessee.\xe2\x80\x9d

  • In the wake of Miami\xe2\x80\x99s crypto boom, Andreessen Horowitz\xe2\x80\x99s Miami office has quietly closed.

  • The \xe2\x80\x9cfounder mode\xe2\x80\x9d conversation continues. We\xe2\x80\x99re still chatting about it in the Feed Me chat.

  • Later this month, I\xe2\x80\x99m doing a talk with Carlos Quirarte (Pebble Bar, Ray\xe2\x80\x99s) and Erika Ayers Badan (Food52, formerly Barstool) if you want to come \xe2\x80\x94 it\xe2\x80\x99s free!

Feed Me is a reader-supported publication. It\xe2\x80\x99s $7/month, which is 35 cents a day. Usually the good stuff happens below the paywall.

readfeedme.com · by Emily SundbergSep 04, 2024
 
Today\xe2\x80\x99s letter is free, and brought to you by J.Crew. Good morning everyone. I am slightly hungover from an unnecessary (but fun!) extra glass of wine last night at Gage & Tollner . Last night, we had a Feed Me book club for paid readers in Brooklyn thank you everyone who came, and talked about sex, divorce, and dating in New York. I\xe2\x80\x99ve been drinking Magna every time I drink but I ran out\xe2\x80\xa6 The best September issue cover belongs to..J.Crew. J.Crew\xe2\x80\x99s iconic catalog ceased production in 2017, but in a genius marketing move they\xe2\x80\x99ve brought it back. You might be wondering why the brand would spend so much time, money, and production on an analog advertising strategy, but I need to point out a few things. Erika wrote an ode to the characters in the catalog back in May it blew up on Substack. The @lostjcrew account on Instagram has over 80k followers. The comment section on a recent Sprezza post about J.Crew catalogs is a study in the brand itself one of them reads: \xe2\x80\x9cLove how almost all of these are outdoors. In the city, at the beach, on a boat; it\xe2\x80\x99s a stark contrast to today. How many brands today are doing their look books ads against a colored background or a wall with a chair and side table against it? Most of them? I understand that there\xe2\x80\x99s pros to doing it that way but a lot of these older photo books showed you their clothing in action, worn out and about while doing things that pertained to the brands legacy. J Crew was a lifestyle brand and as such the ads reflected that at a time where people weren\xe2\x80\x99t glued to their phones. Look books were reflecting what people did: living life doing things. Today it\xe2\x80\x99s all about a fit pic in a mirror so it\xe2\x80\x99s no surprise we get look books with guys just standing there in a pose; it\xe2\x80\x99s reflective of what people are doing today.\xe2\x80\x9d Brand loyalists in the J.Crew Passport Program will get an issue of the catalog straight to their mailbox, but you can also grab a copy in- store, or request one online. I guess you could also steal your neighbors copy if you see it in your vestibule, but I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t recommend that on the record (although it\xe2\x80\x99s tempting). So now you understand why the medium is important. But let\xe2\x80\x99s get to the message. The first thing you\xe2\x80\x99ll notice about the J.Crew Fall 2024 cover (the one I received there are four collectible versions in circulation) is Demi Moore\xe2\x80\x99s long dark hair, surrounding one of the coolest faces in Hollywood. Inside, an interview with the actor reveals that she has been collecting J.Crew pieces for decades, and her collection includes everything from work boots to oversized sweaters. She even discusses the big secret of her industry: they all live in two worlds. The one that plays out on the red carpet, and the one that happens on the grass. The issue also contains photos that are purely for the campaign it\xe2\x80\x99s up to the reader to make up their stories. The group of strangers (or have they known each other forever?) who ended up in a beach house together. The couple who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep their hands off each other in Central Park. And one of my favorites, a two- page spread of varying J.Crew tags from 1983 to today. The issue also features: 5 dogs 1 slice of New York pizza 2 makeouts 10 barn jackets (I got this one ) 1 boat, 1 truck, and 1 taxi the proper ratio of transportation in one\xe2\x80\x99s life This sweater hasn\xe2\x80\x99t come off my shoulders this week, I wore these heels to my meeting with my money guy on Park Avenue last week and walked out of the office learning that subscription businesses file taxes differently, and this is the shirt I\xe2\x80\x99m wearing at the top of the letter. Miranda July answered your questions! Last night, a group of paid Feed Me readers joined me and my friend Krithika in the (beautiful\xe2\x80\xa6 ) garden behind Warby Parker in Boerum Hill to discuss Miranda July\xe2\x80\x99s All Fours . I was going to try to record our conversation, but decided to keep our conversations off- the- record. Earlier this summer, some of you submitted questions for Miranda that she kindly took the time to answer, so I have those for you today. Cool for those of you who read the book, perhaps interesting for those of you who want to learn more about writing a \xe2\x80\x9cbook of the summer.\xe2\x80\x9d Thank you everyone who took photos last night - I was too excited and forgot to. How has she approached the question of \xe2\x80\x9cso\xe2\x80\xa6 how much of this book is your real experience?\xe2\x80\x9d does it bug her? Does she understand the inclination or resent people for asking? A. I knew there would be this question (I would have the same question as a reader) and I thought, Well rather than resent the conflation and work overtime to make an overt distinction between me and the narrator, perhaps I could share a few details of my position with her. Maybe that\xe2\x80\x99s a more fun way to address this problem, to use it as a tool to help tell the fictional story, to add a little energy. I thought of it like I was generously loaning some parts of myself to my narrator and then doing whatever the hell I wanted with her. Whenever I\xe2\x80\x99m reading \xe2\x80\x9cauto fiction\xe2\x80\x9d (currently If You\xe2\x80\x99re A Girl by Ann Rower) I love thinking This isn\xe2\x80\x99t just some made- up writerly bullshit, this person lived to tell the tale! I wanted that sense of truth and though I knew it had to be fiction, that\xe2\x80\x99s my bag, I also wanted to communicate to the reader that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t just safely dreaming up tall tales from my cozy armchair in my own (more complex, more real- life) way I, too, was in the depths. Does the personal nature of people\xe2\x80\x99s responses to the book change her own perception of the work? A. I do see the book a little differently now but maybe this always happens when the readers finish the book. I guess because it\xe2\x80\x99s only the readers who can place it in now, a shared now try as you might, you can\xe2\x80\x99t do that as just one person. These responses, these stories of how the book has intersected with the reader\xe2\x80\x99s specific lives, is what I hoped for every day of those four years of writing and living and feeling so alone, but placing all my bets on the hunch that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t alone. The motel room setting was SOOO well written and juicy and fun. I loved the details of the tile, the scent, etc etc. what was the inspiration behind having a setting play such a big part in the book? it's almost like another character. and i would genuinely love to know what the most fun part of writing this book was... torment obviously plays a big role in the plot but it seems like a fun book to write (hope this isn't too earnest and dumb, haha) A. It was fun to decorate the hotel room, to write those scenes. I had never done anything like that in real life and it seemed indulgent, like a pleasure I had given myself that was the initial impetus, nothing deeper. It was only much later (yesterday?) that I thought Ohhh\xe2\x80\xa6 .she\xe2\x80\x99s making a home . And maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s a book about making a home, a life for yourself that is so specific that it actually makes no sense to anyone else and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have to. If I had known I was doing that at the start I probably wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have done it, it would have seemed like hard, painful work that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a clue about. Sometimes maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s best, in art and writing, to be as dumb as possible, follow the joy, because at the very least that's real. And there, you\xe2\x80\x99ve taken the first step towards the truth. Remember the scene when they have a brunch for Caro? That\xe2\x80\x99s the only scene where that small character talks and I remember being so surprised by her voice and the things she said, such that it was very easy to write the narrator being taken by surprise. That was fun to write. The tampon scene was too. I wrote that scene bit by bit over about four days, like building a tower that you hope won\xe2\x80\x99t fall before you\xe2\x80\x99re done. I listened to the entire All Fours audiobook in 2 days, was hooked. What was the approach to recording it? On the one hand it\xe2\x80\x99s just a straight reading of the book, but the performance of certain scenes felt so considered, and the vocal changes for the different characters was so fun. A. A thing I did with All Fours that I had never done before was that for the final three drafts - the stage where you are just making tiny (but I would say important) changes I read the whole book aloud. To no one; just alone in my studio, about 50 pages a day. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t give great performances or anything, I just read like this so that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t miss anything. Like if it didn\xe2\x80\x99t roll off the tongue, I paused to study why that was, if it was worth it. I remember telling the producer beforehand that \xe2\x80\x9cI always read very deadpan, I don\xe2\x80\x99t do voices.\xe2\x80\x9d But then about thirty minutes in, I stopped mid- sentence and said from the booth \xe2\x80\x9cHold on have I been doing voices?? This is a big problem because I can\xe2\x80\x99t keep this up, and how will I come up with new voices for each new character?!\xe2\x80\x9d But it was too late now, I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to have to go back to the start, and also\xe2\x80\xa6 the voices were just coming. Not like big actor- y characters (I mean, you tell me actually; I'm just going off memory here) but each character\xe2\x80\x99s voice seemed fairly obvious. Maybe from those three times reading it aloud. What was pinned to the \xe2\x80\x9cgarage wall\xe2\x80\x9d when you worked on this project? Other than the wedding portrait (I read the New Yorker piece ). A. I don\xe2\x80\x99t actually have a lot of visual stuff around when I\xe2\x80\x99m writing fiction. The wedding portrait\xe2\x80\xa6 that\xe2\x80\x99s the cover of an iBook that the journalist spied, but that shelf is a bit of a time capsule. Actually the whole studio is in a way, a place I\xe2\x80\x99ve worked for 20 years but don\xe2\x80\x99t spend a lot of time decorating. But during those four years of writing I had a rolling rack of clothes costumes that I would sometimes change into when I wanted a writing break. I\xe2\x80\x99d put on an outfit that felt inspirational and then I'd move my body. Sometimes I\xe2\x80\x99d have to watch a dance video first, something on IG Sharon Eyal, Storyboard P to remember what dance was (Oh right, you can move any way, you can find a new way to move) and then I\xe2\x80\x99d put on some music and give it a whirl. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t think that much about the relationship between these breaks and dance in the book, but I guess that\xe2\x80\x99s the closest thing I have to the narrator\xe2\x80\x99s clues pinned to the wall. Why include Kris? A. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want the change in the marriage to solve all her problems. At best I think a change in structure like that allows you to see yourself more clearly and what you see is kind of sobering. Not wrong, still interesting and often exciting, but suddenly the scope of the issue changes and the clarity can be a little hellish at first, before the new perspective has been integrated. That\xe2\x80\x99s real life to me, and I don\xe2\x80\x99t think it ever ends and... it\xe2\x80\x99s a human right! Taking away someone\xe2\x80\x99s right to transform is such a crime. Also the strangeness of the first new relationship after a very long marriage ends or changes that\xe2\x80\x99s the sort of thing I would have wanted to read about in my early forties; it was something I couldn\xe2\x80\x99t really conceive of. If we had a water cooler, I\xe2\x80\x99d talk to you about: Back in the spring, I got breakfast with Melanie Masarin and she told me she was developing a new Ghia product. Le Fizz, a bubble orange blossom- meets strawberry bottle, launched today . I texted Melanie about how she imagines people drinking the bottles (which I imagine will end up on many a chic Thanksgiving bar this fall) and she said, \xe2\x80\x9cBringing a bottle of Ghia and including non- alcoholic options is \xe2\x80\x9cvery mindful\xe2\x80\x9d and we\xe2\x80\x99re excited to have a bottle that is ready- to- share, with only good things in it, for the price of bottle of wine. It\xe2\x80\x99s also tannic, tangy, but not bitter, while still having no added sugar something very important to me so I think a lot of people who didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the taste of our initial flavor will really enjoy this one! It hits the spot when you\xe2\x80\x99d be craving wine but it\xe2\x80\x99s absolutely not trying to be wine or non- alcoholic wine.\xe2\x80\x9d I also heard a rumor that the Ghia team hand- labeled 12,000 bottles to bring this launch to life not easy launching a business! Chlo\xc3\xab Sevigny is on the cover of The Cut . Emily Gould interviewed her ( \xe2\x80\x9cI try to fill my life with other things I can do that help keep those juices flowing and engaged to satisfy my urges and also just to be busy.\xe2\x80\x9d ), and Nadia Lee Cohen shot the photos. Miami\xe2\x80\x99s swanky restaurant and members\xe2\x80\x99 club ($4,300 year), Casa Tua, opened in New York at The Surrey uptown. artnet\xe2\x80\x99s Annie Armstrong interviewed Maya Fuchs- Bortolami who will be helping to curate the art there. Haliey Welch, AKA Hawk Tuah Girl, launched a podcast. It\xe2\x80\x99s called Talk Tuah. Puck\xe2\x80\x99s Lauren Sherman spoke to Emily Oberg about the Sporty and Rich newsletter I wrote last week and almost gave me a heart attack. This album fucks. The business of mail- order birth control is booming as abortion restrictions become tighter in the US. \xe2\x80\x9cTelehealth service Nurx saw a 1,700% increase in the number of emergency contraception requests, over half of which came from states in the South like Texas, Florida and Tennessee.\xe2\x80\x9d In the wake of Miami\xe2\x80\x99s crypto boom, Andreessen Horowitz\xe2\x80\x99s Miami office has quietly closed. The \xe2\x80\x9cfounder mode\xe2\x80\x9d conversation continues. We\xe2\x80\x99re still chatting about it in the Feed Me chat. Later this month, I\xe2\x80\x99m doing a talk with Carlos Quirarte (Pebble Bar, Ray\xe2\x80\x99s) and Erika Ayers Badan (Food52, formerly Barstool) if you want to come it\xe2\x80\x99s free! Feed Me is a reader- supported publication. It\xe2\x80\x99s $7 month, which is 35 cents a day. Usually the good stuff happens below the paywall.  \xe2\x97\x86
Today\xe2\x80\x99s letter is free, and brought to you by J.Crew. Good morning everyone. I am slightly hungover from an unnecessary (but fun!) extra glass of wine last night at Gage & Tollner . Last night, we had a Feed Me book club for paid readers in Brooklyn thank you everyone who came, and talked about sex, divorce, and dating in New York. I\xe2\x80\x99ve been drinking Magna every time I drink but I ran out\xe2\x80\xa6 The best September issue cover belongs to..J.Crew. J.Crew\xe2\x80\x99s iconic catalog ceased production in 2017, but in a genius marketing move they\xe2\x80\x99ve brought it back. You might be wondering why the brand would spend so much time, money, and production on an analog advertising strategy, but I need to point out a few things. Erika wrote an ode to the characters in the catalog back in May it blew up on Substack. The @lostjcrew account on Instagram has over 80k followers. The comment section on a recent Sprezza post about J.Crew catalogs is a study in the brand itself one of them reads: \xe2\x80\x9cLove how almost all of these are outdoors. In the city, at the beach, on a boat; it\xe2\x80\x99s a stark contrast to today. How many brands today are doing their look books ads against a colored background or a wall with a chair and side table against it? Most of them? I understand that there\xe2\x80\x99s pros to doing it that way but a lot of these older photo books showed you their clothing in action, worn out and about while doing things that pertained to the brands legacy. J Crew was a lifestyle brand and as such the ads reflected that at a time where people weren\xe2\x80\x99t glued to their phones. Look books were reflecting what people did: living life doing things. Today it\xe2\x80\x99s all about a fit pic in a mirror so it\xe2\x80\x99s no surprise we get look books with guys just standing there in a pose; it\xe2\x80\x99s reflective of what people are doing today.\xe2\x80\x9d Brand loyalists in the J.Crew Passport Program will get an issue of the catalog straight to their mailbox, but you can also grab a copy in- store, or request one online. I guess you could also steal your neighbors copy if you see it in your vestibule, but I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t recommend that on the record (although it\xe2\x80\x99s tempting). So now you understand why the medium is important. But let\xe2\x80\x99s get to the message. The first thing you\xe2\x80\x99ll notice about the J.Crew Fall 2024 cover (the one I received there are four collectible versions in circulation) is Demi Moore\xe2\x80\x99s long dark hair, surrounding one of the coolest faces in Hollywood. Inside, an interview with the actor reveals that she has been collecting J.Crew pieces for decades, and her collection includes everything from work boots to oversized sweaters. She even discusses the big secret of her industry: they all live in two worlds. The one that plays out on the red carpet, and the one that happens on the grass. The issue also contains photos that are purely for the campaign it\xe2\x80\x99s up to the reader to make up their stories. The group of strangers (or have they known each other forever?) who ended up in a beach house together. The couple who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep their hands off each other in Central Park. And one of my favorites, a two- page spread of varying J.Crew tags from 1983 to today. The issue also features: 5 dogs 1 slice of New York pizza 2 makeouts 10 barn jackets (I got this one ) 1 boat, 1 truck, and 1 taxi the proper ratio of transportation in one\xe2\x80\x99s life This sweater hasn\xe2\x80\x99t come off my shoulders this week, I wore these heels to my meeting with my money guy on Park Avenue last week and walked out of the office learning that subscription businesses file taxes differently, and this is the shirt I\xe2\x80\x99m wearing at the top of the letter. Miranda July answered your questions! Last night, a group of paid Feed Me readers joined me and my friend Krithika in the (beautiful\xe2\x80\xa6 ) garden behind Warby Parker in Boerum Hill to discuss Miranda July\xe2\x80\x99s All Fours . I was going to try to record our conversation, but decided to keep our conversations off- the- record. Earlier this summer, some of you submitted questions for Miranda that she kindly took the time to answer, so I have those for you today. Cool for those of you who read the book, perhaps interesting for those of you who want to learn more about writing a \xe2\x80\x9cbook of the summer.\xe2\x80\x9d Thank you everyone who took photos last night - I was too excited and forgot to. How has she approached the question of \xe2\x80\x9cso\xe2\x80\xa6 how much of this book is your real experience?\xe2\x80\x9d does it bug her? Does she understand the inclination or resent people for asking? A. I knew there would be this question (I would have the same question as a reader) and I thought, Well rather than resent the conflation and work overtime to make an overt distinction between me and the narrator, perhaps I could share a few details of my position with her. Maybe that\xe2\x80\x99s a more fun way to address this problem, to use it as a tool to help tell the fictional story, to add a little energy. I thought of it like I was generously loaning some parts of myself to my narrator and then doing whatever the hell I wanted with her. Whenever I\xe2\x80\x99m reading \xe2\x80\x9cauto fiction\xe2\x80\x9d (currently If You\xe2\x80\x99re A Girl by Ann Rower) I love thinking This isn\xe2\x80\x99t just some made- up writerly bullshit, this person lived to tell the tale! I wanted that sense of truth and though I knew it had to be fiction, that\xe2\x80\x99s my bag, I also wanted to communicate to the reader that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t just safely dreaming up tall tales from my cozy armchair in my own (more complex, more real- life) way I, too, was in the depths. Does the personal nature of people\xe2\x80\x99s responses to the book change her own perception of the work? A. I do see the book a little differently now but maybe this always happens when the readers finish the book. I guess because it\xe2\x80\x99s only the readers who can place it in now, a shared now try as you might, you can\xe2\x80\x99t do that as just one person. These responses, these stories of how the book has intersected with the reader\xe2\x80\x99s specific lives, is what I hoped for every day of those four years of writing and living and feeling so alone, but placing all my bets on the hunch that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t alone. The motel room setting was SOOO well written and juicy and fun. I loved the details of the tile, the scent, etc etc. what was the inspiration behind having a setting play such a big part in the book? it's almost like another character. and i would genuinely love to know what the most fun part of writing this book was... torment obviously plays a big role in the plot but it seems like a fun book to write (hope this isn't too earnest and dumb, haha) A. It was fun to decorate the hotel room, to write those scenes. I had never done anything like that in real life and it seemed indulgent, like a pleasure I had given myself that was the initial impetus, nothing deeper. It was only much later (yesterday?) that I thought Ohhh\xe2\x80\xa6 .she\xe2\x80\x99s making a home . And maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s a book about making a home, a life for yourself that is so specific that it actually makes no sense to anyone else and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have to. If I had known I was doing that at the start I probably wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have done it, it would have seemed like hard, painful work that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a clue about. Sometimes maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s best, in art and writing, to be as dumb as possible, follow the joy, because at the very least that's real. And there, you\xe2\x80\x99ve taken the first step towards the truth. Remember the scene when they have a brunch for Caro? That\xe2\x80\x99s the only scene where that small character talks and I remember being so surprised by her voice and the things she said, such that it was very easy to write the narrator being taken by surprise. That was fun to write. The tampon scene was too. I wrote that scene bit by bit over about four days, like building a tower that you hope won\xe2\x80\x99t fall before you\xe2\x80\x99re done. I listened to the entire All Fours audiobook in 2 days, was hooked. What was the approach to recording it? On the one hand it\xe2\x80\x99s just a straight reading of the book, but the performance of certain scenes felt so considered, and the vocal changes for the different characters was so fun. A. A thing I did with All Fours that I had never done before was that for the final three drafts - the stage where you are just making tiny (but I would say important) changes I read the whole book aloud. To no one; just alone in my studio, about 50 pages a day. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t give great performances or anything, I just read like this so that I didn\xe2\x80\x99t miss anything. Like if it didn\xe2\x80\x99t roll off the tongue, I paused to study why that was, if it was worth it. I remember telling the producer beforehand that \xe2\x80\x9cI always read very deadpan, I don\xe2\x80\x99t do voices.\xe2\x80\x9d But then about thirty minutes in, I stopped mid- sentence and said from the booth \xe2\x80\x9cHold on have I been doing voices?? This is a big problem because I can\xe2\x80\x99t keep this up, and how will I come up with new voices for each new character?!\xe2\x80\x9d But it was too late now, I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to have to go back to the start, and also\xe2\x80\xa6 the voices were just coming. Not like big actor- y characters (I mean, you tell me actually; I'm just going off memory here) but each character\xe2\x80\x99s voice seemed fairly obvious. Maybe from those three times reading it aloud. What was pinned to the \xe2\x80\x9cgarage wall\xe2\x80\x9d when you worked on this project? Other than the wedding portrait (I read the New Yorker piece ). A. I don\xe2\x80\x99t actually have a lot of visual stuff around when I\xe2\x80\x99m writing fiction. The wedding portrait\xe2\x80\xa6 that\xe2\x80\x99s the cover of an iBook that the journalist spied, but that shelf is a bit of a time capsule. Actually the whole studio is in a way, a place I\xe2\x80\x99ve worked for 20 years but don\xe2\x80\x99t spend a lot of time decorating. But during those four years of writing I had a rolling rack of clothes costumes that I would sometimes change into when I wanted a writing break. I\xe2\x80\x99d put on an outfit that felt inspirational and then I'd move my body. Sometimes I\xe2\x80\x99d have to watch a dance video first, something on IG Sharon Eyal, Storyboard P to remember what dance was (Oh right, you can move any way, you can find a new way to move) and then I\xe2\x80\x99d put on some music and give it a whirl. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t think that much about the relationship between these breaks and dance in the book, but I guess that\xe2\x80\x99s the closest thing I have to the narrator\xe2\x80\x99s clues pinned to the wall. Why include Kris? A. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t want the change in the marriage to solve all her problems. At best I think a change in structure like that allows you to see yourself more clearly and what you see is kind of sobering. Not wrong, still interesting and often exciting, but suddenly the scope of the issue changes and the clarity can be a little hellish at first, before the new perspective has been integrated. That\xe2\x80\x99s real life to me, and I don\xe2\x80\x99t think it ever ends and... it\xe2\x80\x99s a human right! Taking away someone\xe2\x80\x99s right to transform is such a crime. Also the strangeness of the first new relationship after a very long marriage ends or changes that\xe2\x80\x99s the sort of thing I would have wanted to read about in my early forties; it was something I couldn\xe2\x80\x99t really conceive of. If we had a water cooler, I\xe2\x80\x99d talk to you about: Back in the spring, I got breakfast with Melanie Masarin and she told me she was developing a new Ghia product. Le Fizz, a bubble orange blossom- meets strawberry bottle, launched today . I texted Melanie about how she imagines people drinking the bottles (which I imagine will end up on many a chic Thanksgiving bar this fall) and she said, \xe2\x80\x9cBringing a bottle of Ghia and including non- alcoholic options is \xe2\x80\x9cvery mindful\xe2\x80\x9d and we\xe2\x80\x99re excited to have a bottle that is ready- to- share, with only good things in it, for the price of bottle of wine. It\xe2\x80\x99s also tannic, tangy, but not bitter, while still having no added sugar something very important to me so I think a lot of people who didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the taste of our initial flavor will really enjoy this one! It hits the spot when you\xe2\x80\x99d be craving wine but it\xe2\x80\x99s absolutely not trying to be wine or non- alcoholic wine.\xe2\x80\x9d I also heard a rumor that the Ghia team hand- labeled 12,000 bottles to bring this launch to life not easy launching a business!
